[[arz:نيجورو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Negoroji01s3200.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingi-Shingon|WD=Q10307059}}|prefecture={{ill|Iwade|WD=Q579623}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.28715|N|135.31665|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Japan's Top 100 Cherry Blossom Spots|WD=Q10312216}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1552446]] Wikidata name: Negoro-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Negoro-ji
根来寺 | native_name = | image = Negoroji01s3200.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Pagoda and Hondō of Negoro-ji | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = Negoro-ji | map_relief = 1 | location =  2286 Negoro, Iwade-shi, Wakayama-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|13.76|N|135|18|59.96|E|scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = Shingi-Shingon-shu | deity = {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}} | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = functioning | website = | founded_by = {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} | year_completed = 1130 | designation1 = National Treasure of Japan | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Negoro-ji'''|根来寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the city of {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Surrounded by the sacred peaks of the {{ill|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|en|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|WD=Q11417836|lt=Katsuragi Mountains}}, the temple grounds were designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] and a [[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 2007.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164691 |title= 根来寺境内|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==History== In the latter half of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, from 1131, the priest {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} became head of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon sect}} on {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} and attempted to reform the sect by reuniting the Ōno (小野) and Hirosawa (広沢) branches. He also attempted to assert the authority of Mount Kōya over the temple's metropolitan headquarters at [[Tō-ji]] in Kyoto.{{sfn|Inagaki|2004|p=104}} More controversially, he also attempted to introduce elements from {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|WD=Q732344|lt=Pure Land}} Buddhism into Shingon orthodoxy, including a new ritual called the ''{{nihongo|himitsu nembutsu|秘密念仏|esoteric nembutsu|}}''. These reforms led to animosity form various reactionary political factions within the Shingon hierarchy, and facing calls for his expulsion, he resigned his posts in 1135 and retired to the chapel of Mitsugon-in (密厳院).{{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=244}} However, the animosity against him continued, and after armed followers of the other factions burned down his residence in 1140, he fled further south into the mountains of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} to an estate which he had received in 1132 from ex-{{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} called Ichijō-zan Daidenpon Negoro-ji. He died at this location in 1143 {{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=245}} and one of his disciples, Raiyū (頼瑜, 1226–1304) moved the Daidenbō-in and the Mitsugon-in chapels from Mount Kōya to Negoro-ji in 1288 and established the independence of a new school called {{nihongo|'''Shingi Shingon'''|新義真言宗|New Shingon}}. During the height of its influence in the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple grew to become a huge religious city, with some 450 temples or chapels (2700 by some accounts) on the mountainside, controlling estates with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of 720,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' and with some 10,000 ''{{ill|Negoro-shū|en|Negoro-shū|WD=Q10351965|lt=Negoro-shū}}'' soldiers. Many of these soldiers were armed with [[matchlock]] rifles, which had recently been introduced to Japan. The temple was thus equivalent to a major [[daimyo|Sengoku daimyō]] in terms of economic and military might. The temple enjoyed a good relationship with {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, having assisted him during the {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|WD=Q859815|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji War}} from 1570 to 1580 and with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} during the {{ill|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|en|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|WD=Q1353633|lt=Battle of Komaki and Nagakute}} in 1584; however, it earned the enmity of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, who launched an invasion of Kii Province in 1585. During the {{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} most of the temple was burned down. Since the temple surrendered without resistance, its burning has been subject to some controversy. It is not clear if the temple was burned on Hideyoshi's orders, by its defenders, or as an act of arson by the attackers. The main surviving structure, the "Daidenhōdō" was dismantled and taken away for use as the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} of Tenshō-ji, which Hideyoshi planned as Nobunaga's mausoleum in Funaokayama, Kyoto. However, Tenshō-ji was never built. Following the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}} in 1615 and the destruction of the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi clan}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated the buildings of Shoun-ji, a temple which had been built by Hideyoshi to mourn his infant son Tsurumatsu, to Negoro-ji, which had begun to rebuild. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple was patronized by the {{ill|Kishū Tokugawa family|en|Kishū Tokugawa family|WD=Q11605046|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan}} and continued to rebuild. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted on the temple grounds from 1976, and excavation also unearthed numerous relics such as pottery, lacquerware, Buddhist implements, and weapons. These relics are stored and exhibited at the "Iwade Civic Museum" built on the premises. However, the grounds of the temple are increasing threatened by urban encroachment. ==Cultural Properties== ===Buildings=== ; {{nihongo|Daitō|大塔||}}, National Treasure This is the largest [[:en:Tahō-tō|Tahō-tō]]-style [[Japanese pagoda|Pagoda]] in Japan, with a height of 40 meters and a width of 15 meters. It houses a statue of the {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Vairocana}} Buddha inside a circle of 12 pillars. It is believed to have started construction around 1480, and was completed around 1547. based on a record found within the structure during modern dismantling and repairs. The structure also has bullet holes from the attack on the temple by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1585.The structure was designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}} in 1899{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2865|title=根来寺多宝塔(大塔)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daishi-dō|大塔||}}, Important Cultural Property Along with the Daitō, this three by three bay structure is one of the few in the temple to ave survived in 1585 destruction of the temple. It is estimated to have been built around 1391 from the inscription on the principal image, a statue of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi}}. It was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1944.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2866|title=根来寺大師堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daidenhō-dō|大伝法堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was rebuilt in 1824 by local carpenters with the assistance of craftsmen from Osaka and {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo}}. It is a large three by two bay structure with a large open interior space, and a tile roof.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005099|title=大伝法堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} The Daidenhō-dō contains three statues: a 3.5 meter seated {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}}, with a 3.43 meter seated {{ill|Vajrasattva|en|Vajrasattva|WD=Q2380086|lt=Kongosatta}} on the left and a 3.3 meter seated {{ill|Usnisavijaya|en|Usnisavijaya|WD=Q3552695|lt=Sonshō Butchō}} on the right. These states were completed between 1387 and 1405 and are thus much older than the building itself. The grouping is very unusual, and statues of Sonshō Butchō are also extremely rare in Japan. The group was designated an Important Cultural Property in 1994.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5138|title=木造尊勝仏頂坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5136|title=木造大日如来坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5137|title=木造金剛薩〓坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Kōmyōshingon-den|光明真言殿||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was reconstructed in 1801 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005100|title=光明真言殿|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daimon|大門||}}, Important Cultural Property The main gate of the temple was reconstructed in 1845 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005101|title=大門|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Fudō-dō|不動堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This chapel dedicated to [[Acala|Fudō-myōō]] was reconstructed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005102|title=不動堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Gyōja-dō|行者堂||}}, |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Shōten-dō|聖天堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Negoroji01s3200.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingi-Shingon|WD=Q10307059}}|prefecture={{ill|Iwade|WD=Q579623}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.28715|N|135.31665|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Japan's Top 100 Cherry Blossom Spots|WD=Q10312216}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1552446]] Wikidata name: Negoro-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Negoro-ji
根来寺 | native_name = | image = Negoroji01s3200.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Pagoda and Hondō of Negoro-ji | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = Negoro-ji | map_relief = 1 | location =  2286 Negoro, Iwade-shi, Wakayama-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|13.76|N|135|18|59.96|E|scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = Shingi-Shingon-shu | deity = {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}} | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = functioning | website = | founded_by = {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} | year_completed = 1130 | designation1 = National Treasure of Japan | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Negoro-ji'''|根来寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the city of {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Surrounded by the sacred peaks of the {{ill|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|en|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|WD=Q11417836|lt=Katsuragi Mountains}}, the temple grounds were designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] and a [[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 2007.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164691 |title= 根来寺境内|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==History== In the latter half of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, from 1131, the priest {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} became head of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon sect}} on {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} and attempted to reform the sect by reuniting the Ōno (小野) and Hirosawa (広沢) branches. He also attempted to assert the authority of Mount Kōya over the temple's metropolitan headquarters at [[Tō-ji]] in Kyoto.{{sfn|Inagaki|2004|p=104}} More controversially, he also attempted to introduce elements from {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|WD=Q732344|lt=Pure Land}} Buddhism into Shingon orthodoxy, including a new ritual called the ''{{nihongo|himitsu nembutsu|秘密念仏|esoteric nembutsu|}}''. These reforms led to animosity form various reactionary political factions within the Shingon hierarchy, and facing calls for his expulsion, he resigned his posts in 1135 and retired to the chapel of Mitsugon-in (密厳院).{{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=244}} However, the animosity against him continued, and after armed followers of the other factions burned down his residence in 1140, he fled further south into the mountains of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} to an estate which he had received in 1132 from ex-{{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} called Ichijō-zan Daidenpon Negoro-ji. He died at this location in 1143 {{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=245}} and one of his disciples, Raiyū (頼瑜, 1226–1304) moved the Daidenbō-in and the Mitsugon-in chapels from Mount Kōya to Negoro-ji in 1288 and established the independence of a new school called {{nihongo|'''Shingi Shingon'''|新義真言宗|New Shingon}}. During the height of its influence in the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple grew to become a huge religious city, with some 450 temples or chapels (2700 by some accounts) on the mountainside, controlling estates with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of 720,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' and with some 10,000 ''{{ill|Negoro-shū|en|Negoro-shū|WD=Q10351965|lt=Negoro-shū}}'' soldiers. Many of these soldiers were armed with [[matchlock]] rifles, which had recently been introduced to Japan. The temple was thus equivalent to a major [[daimyo|Sengoku daimyō]] in terms of economic and military might. The temple enjoyed a good relationship with {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, having assisted him during the {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|WD=Q859815|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji War}} from 1570 to 1580 and with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} during the {{ill|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|en|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|WD=Q1353633|lt=Battle of Komaki and Nagakute}} in 1584; however, it earned the enmity of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, who launched an invasion of Kii Province in 1585. During the {{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} most of the temple was burned down. Since the temple surrendered without resistance, its burning has been subject to some controversy. It is not clear if the temple was burned on Hideyoshi's orders, by its defenders, or as an act of arson by the attackers. The main surviving structure, the "Daidenhōdō" was dismantled and taken away for use as the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} of Tenshō-ji, which Hideyoshi planned as Nobunaga's mausoleum in Funaokayama, Kyoto. However, Tenshō-ji was never built. Following the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}} in 1615 and the destruction of the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi clan}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated the buildings of Shoun-ji, a temple which had been built by Hideyoshi to mourn his infant son Tsurumatsu, to Negoro-ji, which had begun to rebuild. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple was patronized by the {{ill|Kishū Tokugawa family|en|Kishū Tokugawa family|WD=Q11605046|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan}} and continued to rebuild. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted on the temple grounds from 1976, and excavation also unearthed numerous relics such as pottery, lacquerware, Buddhist implements, and weapons. These relics are stored and exhibited at the "Iwade Civic Museum" built on the premises. However, the grounds of the temple are increasing threatened by urban encroachment. ==Cultural Properties== ===Buildings=== ; {{nihongo|Daitō|大塔||}}, National Treasure This is the largest [[:en:Tahō-tō|Tahō-tō]]-style [[Japanese pagoda|Pagoda]] in Japan, with a height of 40 meters and a width of 15 meters. It houses a statue of the {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Vairocana}} Buddha inside a circle of 12 pillars. It is believed to have started construction around 1480, and was completed around 1547. based on a record found within the structure during modern dismantling and repairs. The structure also has bullet holes from the attack on the temple by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1585.The structure was designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}} in 1899{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2865|title=根来寺多宝塔(大塔)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daishi-dō|大塔||}}, Important Cultural Property Along with the Daitō, this three by three bay structure is one of the few in the temple to ave survived in 1585 destruction of the temple. It is estimated to have been built around 1391 from the inscription on the principal image, a statue of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi}}. It was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1944.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2866|title=根来寺大師堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daidenhō-dō|大伝法堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was rebuilt in 1824 by local carpenters with the assistance of craftsmen from Osaka and {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo}}. It is a large three by two bay structure with a large open interior space, and a tile roof.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005099|title=大伝法堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} The Daidenhō-dō contains three statues: a 3.5 meter seated {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}}, with a 3.43 meter seated {{ill|Vajrasattva|en|Vajrasattva|WD=Q2380086|lt=Kongosatta}} on the left and a 3.3 meter seated {{ill|Usnisavijaya|en|Usnisavijaya|WD=Q3552695|lt=Sonshō Butchō}} on the right. These states were completed between 1387 and 1405 and are thus much older than the building itself. The grouping is very unusual, and statues of Sonshō Butchō are also extremely rare in Japan. The group was designated an Important Cultural Property in 1994.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5138|title=木造尊勝仏頂坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5136|title=木造大日如来坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5137|title=木造金剛薩〓坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Kōmyōshingon-den|光明真言殿||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was reconstructed in 1801 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005100|title=光明真言殿|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daimon|大門||}}, Important Cultural Property The main gate of the temple was reconstructed in 1845 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005101|title=大門|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Fudō-dō|不動堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This chapel dedicated to [[Acala|Fudō-myōō]] was reconstructed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005102|title=不動堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Gyōja-dō|行者堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005103|title=行者堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Shōten-dō|聖天堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005104|title=聖天堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==Gallery== Negoroji05s3200.jpg|Daimon Negorozi_11.jpg|Fudō-dō Negorozi_12.jpg|Fudō-dō interior Negoroji04s3200.jpg|Daidenhō-dō Negoroji01s3200.jpg|Daitō Pagoda Negoroji02s3872.jpg|Daishi-dō Negoroji Kogyodaishi Mausoleum.jpg|Oku-no-in Negoroji11s3200.jpg|Kōmyōshingon-den Negoroji25s3872.jpg|Gyōja-dō Negoroji14s3872.jpg|Gardens ==See also== *{{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|WD=Q6573290|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)}} *{{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)|WD=Q17051147|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)}} *[[:en:List of National Treasures of 根来寺 | native_name = | image = Negoroji01s3200.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Pagoda and Hondō of Negoro-ji | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = Negoro-ji | map_relief = 1 | location =  2286 Negoro, Iwade-shi, Wakayama-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|13.76|N|135|18|59.96|E|scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = Shingi-Shingon-shu | deity = {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}} | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = functioning | website = | founded_by = {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} | year_completed = 1130 | designation1 = National Treasure of Japan | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Negoro-ji'''|根来寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the city of {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Surrounded by the sacred peaks of the {{ill|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|en|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|WD=Q11417836|lt=Katsuragi Mountains}}, the temple grounds were designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] and a [[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 2007.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164691 |title= 根来寺境内|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==History== In the latter half of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, from 1131, the priest {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} became head of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon sect}} on {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} and attempted to reform the sect by reuniting the Ōno (小野) and Hirosawa (広沢) branches. He also attempted to assert the authority of Mount Kōya over the temple's metropolitan headquarters at [[Tō-ji]] in Kyoto.{{sfn|Inagaki|2004|p=104}} More controversially, he also attempted to introduce elements from {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|WD=Q732344|lt=Pure Land}} Buddhism into Shingon orthodoxy, including a new ritual called the ''{{nihongo|himitsu nembutsu|秘密念仏|esoteric nembutsu|}}''. These reforms led to animosity form various reactionary political factions within the Shingon hierarchy, and facing calls for his expulsion, he resigned his posts in 1135 and retired to the chapel of Mitsugon-in (密厳院).{{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=244}} However, the animosity against him continued, and after armed followers of the other factions burned down his residence in 1140, he fled further south into the mountains of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} to an estate which he had received in 1132 from ex-{{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} called Ichijō-zan Daidenpon Negoro-ji. He died at this location in 1143 {{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=245}} and one of his disciples, Raiyū (頼瑜, 1226–1304) moved the Daidenbō-in and the Mitsugon-in chapels from Mount Kōya to Negoro-ji in 1288 and established the independence of a new school called {{nihongo|'''Shingi Shingon'''|新義真言宗|New Shingon}}. During the height of its influence in the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple grew to become a huge religious city, with some 450 temples or chapels (2700 by some accounts) on the mountainside, controlling estates with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of 720,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' and with some 10,000 ''{{ill|Negoro-shū|en|Negoro-shū|WD=Q10351965|lt=Negoro-shū}}'' soldiers. Many of these soldiers were armed with [[matchlock]] rifles, which had recently been introduced to Japan. The temple was thus equivalent to a major [[daimyo|Sengoku daimyō]] in terms of economic and military might. The temple enjoyed a good relationship with {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, having assisted him during the {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|WD=Q859815|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji War}} from 1570 to 1580 and with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} during the {{ill|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|en|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|WD=Q1353633|lt=Battle of Komaki and Nagakute}} in 1584; however, it earned the enmity of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, who launched an invasion of Kii Province in 1585. During the {{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} most of the temple was burned down. Since the temple surrendered without resistance, its burning has been subject to some controversy. It is not clear if the temple was burned on Hideyoshi's orders, by its defenders, or as an act of arson by the attackers. The main surviving structure, the "Daidenhōdō" was dismantled and taken away for use as the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} of Tenshō-ji, which Hideyoshi planned as Nobunaga's mausoleum in Funaokayama, Kyoto. However, Tenshō-ji was never built. Following the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}} in 1615 and the destruction of the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi clan}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated the buildings of Shoun-ji, a temple which had been built by Hideyoshi to mourn his infant son Tsurumatsu, to Negoro-ji, which had begun to rebuild. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple was patronized by the {{ill|Kishū Tokugawa family|en|Kishū Tokugawa family|WD=Q11605046|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan}} and continued to rebuild. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted on the temple grounds from 1976, and excavation also unearthed numerous relics such as pottery, lacquerware, Buddhist implements, and weapons. These relics are stored and exhibited at the "Iwade Civic Museum" built on the premises. However, the grounds of the temple are increasing threatened by urban encroachment. ==Cultural Properties== ===Buildings=== ; {{nihongo|Daitō|大塔||}}, National Treasure This is the largest [[:en:Tahō-tō|Tahō-tō]]-style [[Japanese pagoda|Pagoda]] in Japan, with a height of 40 meters and a width of 15 meters. It houses a statue of the {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Vairocana}} Buddha inside a circle of 12 pillars. It is believed to have started construction around 1480, and was completed around 1547. based on a record found within the structure during modern dismantling and repairs. The structure also has bullet holes from the attack on the temple by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1585.The structure was designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}} in 1899{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2865|title=根来寺多宝塔(大塔)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daishi-dō|大塔||}}, Important Cultural Property Along with the Daitō, this three by three bay structure is one of the few in the temple to ave survived in 1585 destruction of the temple. It is estimated to have been built around 1391 from the inscription on the principal image, a statue of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi}}. It was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1944.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2866|title=根来寺大師堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daidenhō-dō|大伝法堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was rebuilt in 1824 by local carpenters with the assistance of craftsmen from Osaka and {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo}}. It is a large three by two bay structure with a large open interior space, and a tile roof.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005099|title=大伝法堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} The Daidenhō-dō contains three statues: a 3.5 meter seated {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}}, with a 3.43 meter seated {{ill|Vajrasattva|en|Vajrasattva|WD=Q2380086|lt=Kongosatta}} on the left and a 3.3 meter seated {{ill|Usnisavijaya|en|Usnisavijaya|WD=Q3552695|lt=Sonshō Butchō}} on the right. These states were completed between 1387 and 1405 and are thus much older than the building itself. The grouping is very unusual, and statues of Sonshō Butchō are also extremely rare in Japan. The group was designated an Important Cultural Property in 1994.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5138|title=木造尊勝仏頂坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5136|title=木造大日如来坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5137|title=木造金剛薩〓坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Kōmyōshingon-den|光明真言殿||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was reconstructed in 1801 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005100|title=光明真言殿|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daimon|大門||}}, Important Cultural Property The main gate of the temple was reconstructed in 1845 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005101|title=大門|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Fudō-dō|不動堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This chapel dedicated to [[Acala|Fudō-myōō]] was reconstructed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005102|title=不動堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Gyōja-dō|行者堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005103|title=行者堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Shōten-dō|聖天堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005104|title=聖天堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==Gallery== Negoroji05s3200.jpg|Daimon Negorozi_11.jpg|Fudō-dō Negorozi_12.jpg|Fudō-dō interior Negoroji04s3200.jpg|Daidenhō-dō Negoroji01s3200.jpg|Daitō Pagoda Negoroji02s3872.jpg|Daishi-dō Negoroji Kogyodaishi Mausoleum.jpg|Oku-no-in Negoroji11s3200.jpg|Kōmyōshingon-den Negoroji25s3872.jpg|Gyōja-dō Negoroji14s3872.jpg|Gardens ==See also== *{{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|WD=Q6573290|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)}} *{{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)|WD=Q17051147|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)}} *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (Temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (Temples)]] *{{ill|The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai|en|The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai|WD=Q11656381|lt=The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai}} 根来寺 | native_name = | image = Negoroji01s3200.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Pagoda and Hondō of Negoro-ji | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = Negoro-ji | map_relief = 1 | location =  2286 Negoro, Iwade-shi, Wakayama-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|13.76|N|135|18|59.96|E|scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = Shingi-Shingon-shu | deity = {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}} | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = functioning | website = | founded_by = {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} | year_completed = 1130 | designation1 = National Treasure of Japan | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Negoro-ji'''|根来寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the city of {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Surrounded by the sacred peaks of the {{ill|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|en|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|WD=Q11417836|lt=Katsuragi Mountains}}, the temple grounds were designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] and a [[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 2007.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164691 |title= 根来寺境内|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==History== In the latter half of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, from 1131, the priest {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} became head of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon sect}} on {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} and attempted to reform the sect by reuniting the Ōno (小野) and Hirosawa (広沢) branches. He also attempted to assert the authority of Mount Kōya over the temple's metropolitan headquarters at [[Tō-ji]] in Kyoto.{{sfn|Inagaki|2004|p=104}} More controversially, he also attempted to introduce elements from {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|WD=Q732344|lt=Pure Land}} Buddhism into Shingon orthodoxy, including a new ritual called the ''{{nihongo|himitsu nembutsu|秘密念仏|esoteric nembutsu|}}''. These reforms led to animosity form various reactionary political factions within the Shingon hierarchy, and facing calls for his expulsion, he resigned his posts in 1135 and retired to the chapel of Mitsugon-in (密厳院).{{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=244}} However, the animosity against him continued, and after armed followers of the other factions burned down his residence in 1140, he fled further south into the mountains of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} to an estate which he had received in 1132 from ex-{{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} called Ichijō-zan Daidenpon Negoro-ji. He died at this location in 1143 {{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=245}} and one of his disciples, Raiyū (頼瑜, 1226–1304) moved the Daidenbō-in and the Mitsugon-in chapels from Mount Kōya to Negoro-ji in 1288 and established the independence of a new school called {{nihongo|'''Shingi Shingon'''|新義真言宗|New Shingon}}. During the height of its influence in the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple grew to become a huge religious city, with some 450 temples or chapels (2700 by some accounts) on the mountainside, controlling estates with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of 720,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' and with some 10,000 ''{{ill|Negoro-shū|en|Negoro-shū|WD=Q10351965|lt=Negoro-shū}}'' soldiers. Many of these soldiers were armed with [[matchlock]] rifles, which had recently been introduced to Japan. The temple was thus equivalent to a major [[daimyo|Sengoku daimyō]] in terms of economic and military might. The temple enjoyed a good relationship with {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, having assisted him during the {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|WD=Q859815|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji War}} from 1570 to 1580 and with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} during the {{ill|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|en|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|WD=Q1353633|lt=Battle of Komaki and Nagakute}} in 1584; however, it earned the enmity of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, who launched an invasion of Kii Province in 1585. During the {{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} most of the temple was burned down. Since the temple surrendered without resistance, its burning has been subject to some controversy. It is not clear if the temple was burned on Hideyoshi's orders, by its defenders, or as an act of arson by the attackers. The main surviving structure, the "Daidenhōdō" was dismantled and taken away for use as the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} of Tenshō-ji, which Hideyoshi planned as Nobunaga's mausoleum in Funaokayama, Kyoto. However, Tenshō-ji was never built. Following the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}} in 1615 and the destruction of the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi clan}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated the buildings of Shoun-ji, a temple which had been built by Hideyoshi to mourn his infant son Tsurumatsu, to Negoro-ji, which had begun to rebuild. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple was patronized by the {{ill|Kishū Tokugawa family|en|Kishū Tokugawa family|WD=Q11605046|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan}} and continued to rebuild. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted on the temple grounds from 1976, and excavation also unearthed numerous relics such as pottery, lacquerware, Buddhist implements, and weapons. These relics are stored and exhibited at the "Iwade Civic Museum" built on the premises. However, the grounds of the temple are increasing threatened by urban encroachment. ==Cultural Properties== ===Buildings=== ; {{nihongo|Daitō|大塔||}}, National Treasure This is the largest [[:en:Tahō-tō|Tahō-tō]]-style [[Japanese pagoda|Pagoda]] in Japan, with a height of 40 meters and a width of 15 meters. It houses a statue of the {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Vairocana}} Buddha inside a circle of 12 pillars. It is believed to have started construction around 1480, and was completed around 1547. based on a record found within the structure during modern dismantling and repairs. The structure also has bullet holes from the attack on the temple by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1585.The structure was designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}} in 1899{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2865|title=根来寺多宝塔(大塔)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daishi-dō|大塔||}}, Important Cultural Property Along with the Daitō, this three by three bay structure is one of the few in the temple to ave survived in 1585 destruction of the temple. It is estimated to have been built around 1391 from the inscription on the principal image, a statue of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi}}. It was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1944.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2866|title=根来寺大師堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daidenhō-dō|大伝法堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was rebuilt in 1824 by local carpenters with the assistance of craftsmen from Osaka and {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo}}. It is a large three by two bay structure with a large open interior space, and a tile roof.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005099|title=大伝法堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} The Daidenhō-dō contains three statues: a 3.5 meter seated {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}}, with a 3.43 meter seated {{ill|Vajrasattva|en|Vajrasattva|WD=Q2380086|lt=Kongosatta}} on the left and a 3.3 meter seated {{ill|Usnisavijaya|en|Usnisavijaya|WD=Q3552695|lt=Sonshō Butchō}} on the right. These states were completed between 1387 and 1405 and are thus much older than the building itself. The grouping is very unusual, and statues of Sonshō Butchō are also extremely rare in Japan. The group was designated an Important Cultural Property in 1994.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5138|title=木造尊勝仏頂坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5136|title=木造大日如来坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5137|title=木造金剛薩〓坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Kōmyōshingon-den|光明真言殿||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was reconstructed in 1801 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005100|title=光明真言殿|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daimon|大門||}}, Important Cultural Property The main gate of the temple was reconstructed in 1845 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005101|title=大門|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Fudō-dō|不動堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This chapel dedicated to [[Acala|Fudō-myōō]] was reconstructed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005102|title=不動堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Gyōja-dō|行者堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005103|title=行者堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Shōten-dō|聖天堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005104|title=聖天堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==Gallery== [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Negoroji01s3200.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingi-Shingon|WD=Q10307059}}|prefecture={{ill|Iwade|WD=Q579623}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.28715|N|135.31665|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Japan's Top 100 Cherry Blossom Spots|WD=Q10312216}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Negoro-ji
根来寺 | native_name = | image = Negoroji01s3200.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Pagoda and Hondō of Negoro-ji | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = Negoro-ji | map_relief = 1 | location =  2286 Negoro, Iwade-shi, Wakayama-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|13.76|N|135|18|59.96|E|scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = Shingi-Shingon-shu | deity = {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}} | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = functioning | website = | founded_by = {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} | year_completed = 1130 | designation1 = National Treasure of Japan | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Negoro-ji'''|根来寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the city of {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Surrounded by the sacred peaks of the {{ill|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|en|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|WD=Q11417836|lt=Katsuragi Mountains}}, the temple grounds were designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] and a [[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 2007.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164691 |title= 根来寺境内|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==History== In the latter half of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, from 1131, the priest {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} became head of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon sect}} on {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} and attempted to reform the sect by reuniting the Ōno (小野) and Hirosawa (広沢) branches. He also attempted to assert the authority of Mount Kōya over the temple's metropolitan headquarters at [[Tō-ji]] in Kyoto.{{sfn|Inagaki|2004|p=104}} More controversially, he also attempted to introduce elements from {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|WD=Q732344|lt=Pure Land}} Buddhism into Shingon orthodoxy, including a new ritual called the ''{{nihongo|himitsu nembutsu|秘密念仏|esoteric nembutsu|}}''. These reforms led to animosity form various reactionary political factions within the Shingon hierarchy, and facing calls for his expulsion, he resigned his posts in 1135 and retired to the chapel of Mitsugon-in (密厳院).{{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=244}} However, the animosity against him continued, and after armed followers of the other factions burned down his residence in 1140, he fled further south into the mountains of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} to an estate which he had received in 1132 from ex-{{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} called Ichijō-zan Daidenpon Negoro-ji. He died at this location in 1143 {{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=245}} and one of his disciples, Raiyū (頼瑜, 1226–1304) moved the Daidenbō-in and the Mitsugon-in chapels from Mount Kōya to Negoro-ji in 1288 and established the independence of a new school called {{nihongo|'''Shingi Shingon'''|新義真言宗|New Shingon}}. During the height of its influence in the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple grew to become a huge religious city, with some 450 temples or chapels (2700 by some accounts) on the mountainside, controlling estates with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of 720,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' and with some 10,000 ''{{ill|Negoro-shū|en|Negoro-shū|WD=Q10351965|lt=Negoro-shū}}'' soldiers. Many of these soldiers were armed with [[matchlock]] rifles, which had recently been introduced to Japan. The temple was thus equivalent to a major [[daimyo|Sengoku daimyō]] in terms of economic and military might. The temple enjoyed a good relationship with {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, having assisted him during the {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|WD=Q859815|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji War}} from 1570 to 1580 and with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} during the {{ill|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|en|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|WD=Q1353633|lt=Battle of Komaki and Nagakute}} in 1584; however, it earned the enmity of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, who launched an invasion of Kii Province in 1585. During the {{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} most of the temple was burned down. Since the temple surrendered without resistance, its burning has been subject to some controversy. It is not clear if the temple was burned on Hideyoshi's orders, by its defenders, or as an act of arson by the attackers. The main surviving structure, the "Daidenhōdō" was dismantled and taken away for use as the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} of Tenshō-ji, which Hideyoshi planned as Nobunaga's mausoleum in Funaokayama, Kyoto. However, Tenshō-ji was never built. Following the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}} in 1615 and the destruction of the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi clan}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated the buildings of Shoun-ji, a temple which had been built by Hideyoshi to mourn his infant son Tsurumatsu, to Negoro-ji, which had begun to rebuild. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple was patronized by the {{ill|Kishū Tokugawa family|en|Kishū Tokugawa family|WD=Q11605046|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan}} and continued to rebuild. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted on the temple grounds from 1976, and excavation also unearthed numerous relics such as pottery, lacquerware, Buddhist implements, and weapons. These relics are stored and exhibited at the "Iwade Civic Museum" built on the premises. However, the grounds of the temple are increasing threatened by urban encroachment. ==Cultural Properties== ===Buildings=== ; {{nihongo|Daitō|大塔||}}, National Treasure This is the largest [[:en:Tahō-tō|Tahō-tō]]-style [[Japanese pagoda|Pagoda]] in Japan, with a height of 40 meters and a width of 15 meters. It houses a statue of the {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Vairocana}} Buddha inside a circle of 12 pillars. It is believed to have started construction around 1480, and was completed around 1547. based on a record found within the structure during modern dismantling and repairs. The structure also has bullet holes from the attack on the temple by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1585.The structure was designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}} in 1899{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2865|title=根来寺多宝塔(大塔)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daishi-dō|大塔||}}, Important Cultural Property Along with the Daitō, this three by three bay structure is one of the few in the temple to ave survived in 1585 destruction of the temple. It is estimated to have been built around 1391 from the inscription on the principal image, a statue of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi}}. It was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1944.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2866|title=根来寺大師堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daidenhō-dō|大伝法堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was rebuilt in 1824 by local carpenters with the assistance of craftsmen from Osaka and {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo}}. It is a large three by two bay structure with a large open interior space, and a tile roof.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005099|title=大伝法堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} The Daidenhō-dō contains three statues: a 3.5 meter seated {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}}, with a 3.43 meter seated {{ill|Vajrasattva|en|Vajrasattva|WD=Q2380086|lt=Kongosatta}} on the left and a 3.3 meter seated {{ill|Usnisavijaya|en|Usnisavijaya|WD=Q3552695|lt=Sonshō Butchō}} on the right. These states were completed between 1387 and 1405 and are thus much older than the building itself. The grouping is very unusual, and statues of Sonshō Butchō are also extremely rare in Japan. The group was designated an Important Cultural Property in 1994.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5138|title=木造尊勝仏頂坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5136|title=木造大日如来坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5137|title=木造金剛薩〓坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Kōmyōshingon-den|光明真言殿||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was reconstructed in 1801 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005100|title=光明真言殿|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daimon|大門||}}, Important Cultural Property The main gate of the temple was reconstructed in 1845 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005101|title=大門|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Fudō-dō|不動堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This chapel dedicated to [[Acala|Fudō-myōō]] was reconstructed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005102|title=不動堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Gyōja-dō|行者堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005103|title=行者堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Shōten-dō|聖天堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005104|title=聖天堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==Gallery== [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[co:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] [[arz:نيجورو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Negoroji (Iwade)]][[de:Negoro-ji]][[en:Negoro-ji]][[fa:نگورو-جی]][[fr:Negoro-ji]][[gan:根來寺]][[it:Negoro-ji]][[ja:根来寺]][[zh:根來寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Negoroji01s3200.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingi-Shingon|WD=Q10307059}}|prefecture={{ill|Iwade|WD=Q579623}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.28715|N|135.31665|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Japan's Top 100 Cherry Blossom Spots|WD=Q10312216}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Negoro-ji
根来寺 | native_name = | image = Negoroji01s3200.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Pagoda and Hondō of Negoro-ji | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = Negoro-ji | map_relief = 1 | location =  2286 Negoro, Iwade-shi, Wakayama-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|13.76|N|135|18|59.96|E|scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = Shingi-Shingon-shu | deity = {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}} | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = functioning | website = | founded_by = {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} | year_completed = 1130 | designation1 = National Treasure of Japan | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Negoro-ji'''|根来寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the city of {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Surrounded by the sacred peaks of the {{ill|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|en|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|WD=Q11417836|lt=Katsuragi Mountains}}, the temple grounds were designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] and a [[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 2007.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164691 |title= 根来寺境内|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==History== In the latter half of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, from 1131, the priest {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban}} became head of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon sect}} on {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} and attempted to reform the sect by reuniting the Ōno (小野) and Hirosawa (広沢) branches. He also attempted to assert the authority of Mount Kōya over the temple's metropolitan headquarters at [[Tō-ji]] in Kyoto.{{sfn|Inagaki|2004|p=104}} More controversially, he also attempted to introduce elements from {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|WD=Q732344|lt=Pure Land}} Buddhism into Shingon orthodoxy, including a new ritual called the ''{{nihongo|himitsu nembutsu|秘密念仏|esoteric nembutsu|}}''. These reforms led to animosity form various reactionary political factions within the Shingon hierarchy, and facing calls for his expulsion, he resigned his posts in 1135 and retired to the chapel of Mitsugon-in (密厳院).{{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=244}} However, the animosity against him continued, and after armed followers of the other factions burned down his residence in 1140, he fled further south into the mountains of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} to an estate which he had received in 1132 from ex-{{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|WD=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba}} called Ichijō-zan Daidenpon Negoro-ji. He died at this location in 1143 {{sfn|Giebel|2004|p=245}} and one of his disciples, Raiyū (頼瑜, 1226–1304) moved the Daidenbō-in and the Mitsugon-in chapels from Mount Kōya to Negoro-ji in 1288 and established the independence of a new school called {{nihongo|'''Shingi Shingon'''|新義真言宗|New Shingon}}. During the height of its influence in the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple grew to become a huge religious city, with some 450 temples or chapels (2700 by some accounts) on the mountainside, controlling estates with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of 720,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' and with some 10,000 ''{{ill|Negoro-shū|en|Negoro-shū|WD=Q10351965|lt=Negoro-shū}}'' soldiers. Many of these soldiers were armed with [[matchlock]] rifles, which had recently been introduced to Japan. The temple was thus equivalent to a major [[daimyo|Sengoku daimyō]] in terms of economic and military might. The temple enjoyed a good relationship with {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, having assisted him during the {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji War|WD=Q859815|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji War}} from 1570 to 1580 and with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} during the {{ill|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|en|Battle of Komaki and Nagakute|WD=Q1353633|lt=Battle of Komaki and Nagakute}} in 1584; however, it earned the enmity of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, who launched an invasion of Kii Province in 1585. During the {{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} most of the temple was burned down. Since the temple surrendered without resistance, its burning has been subject to some controversy. It is not clear if the temple was burned on Hideyoshi's orders, by its defenders, or as an act of arson by the attackers. The main surviving structure, the "Daidenhōdō" was dismantled and taken away for use as the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} of Tenshō-ji, which Hideyoshi planned as Nobunaga's mausoleum in Funaokayama, Kyoto. However, Tenshō-ji was never built. Following the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}} in 1615 and the destruction of the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi clan}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} donated the buildings of Shoun-ji, a temple which had been built by Hideyoshi to mourn his infant son Tsurumatsu, to Negoro-ji, which had begun to rebuild. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple was patronized by the {{ill|Kishū Tokugawa family|en|Kishū Tokugawa family|WD=Q11605046|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan}} and continued to rebuild. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted on the temple grounds from 1976, and excavation also unearthed numerous relics such as pottery, lacquerware, Buddhist implements, and weapons. These relics are stored and exhibited at the "Iwade Civic Museum" built on the premises. However, the grounds of the temple are increasing threatened by urban encroachment. ==Cultural Properties== ===Buildings=== ; {{nihongo|Daitō|大塔||}}, National Treasure This is the largest [[:en:Tahō-tō|Tahō-tō]]-style [[Japanese pagoda|Pagoda]] in Japan, with a height of 40 meters and a width of 15 meters. It houses a statue of the {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Vairocana}} Buddha inside a circle of 12 pillars. It is believed to have started construction around 1480, and was completed around 1547. based on a record found within the structure during modern dismantling and repairs. The structure also has bullet holes from the attack on the temple by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1585.The structure was designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}} in 1899{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2865|title=根来寺多宝塔(大塔)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daishi-dō|大塔||}}, Important Cultural Property Along with the Daitō, this three by three bay structure is one of the few in the temple to ave survived in 1585 destruction of the temple. It is estimated to have been built around 1391 from the inscription on the principal image, a statue of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi}}. It was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1944.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2866|title=根来寺大師堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daidenhō-dō|大伝法堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was rebuilt in 1824 by local carpenters with the assistance of craftsmen from Osaka and {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo}}. It is a large three by two bay structure with a large open interior space, and a tile roof.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005099|title=大伝法堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} The Daidenhō-dō contains three statues: a 3.5 meter seated {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}}, with a 3.43 meter seated {{ill|Vajrasattva|en|Vajrasattva|WD=Q2380086|lt=Kongosatta}} on the left and a 3.3 meter seated {{ill|Usnisavijaya|en|Usnisavijaya|WD=Q3552695|lt=Sonshō Butchō}} on the right. These states were completed between 1387 and 1405 and are thus much older than the building itself. The grouping is very unusual, and statues of Sonshō Butchō are also extremely rare in Japan. The group was designated an Important Cultural Property in 1994.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5138|title=木造尊勝仏頂坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5136|title=木造大日如来坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5137|title=木造金剛薩〓坐像|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Kōmyōshingon-den|光明真言殿||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was reconstructed in 1801 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005100|title=光明真言殿|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Daimon|大門||}}, Important Cultural Property The main gate of the temple was reconstructed in 1845 and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005101|title=大門|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Fudō-dō|不動堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This chapel dedicated to [[Acala|Fudō-myōō]] was reconstructed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005102|title=不動堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Gyōja-dō|行者堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005103|title=行者堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ; {{nihongo|Shōten-dō|聖天堂||}}, Important Cultural Property This building was completed in the mid-Edo period and designated 2019{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00005104|title=聖天堂|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==Gallery== Negoroji05s3200.jpg|Daimon Negorozi_11.jpg|Fudō-dō Negorozi_12.jpg|Fudō-dō interior Negoroji04s3200.jpg|Daidenhō-dō Negoroji01s3200.jpg|Daitō Pagoda Negoroji02s3872.jpg|Daishi-dō Negoroji Kogyodaishi Mausoleum.jpg|Oku-no-in Negoroji11s3200.jpg|Kōmyōshingon-den Negoroji25s3872.jpg|Gyōja-dō Negoroji14s3872.jpg|Gardens ==See also== *{{ill|Siege of Negoro-ji|en|Siege of Negoro-ji|WD=Q7510268|lt=Siege of Negoro-ji}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)|WD=Q6573290|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Wakayama)}} *{{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)|WD=Q17051147|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Wakayama)}} *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (Temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (Temples)]] *{{ill|The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai|en|The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai|WD=Q11656381|lt=The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai}} 根来寺 | native_name = | image = Negoroji01s3200.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Pagoda and Hondō of Negoro-ji | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = Negoro-ji | map_relief = 1 | location =  2286 Negoro, Iwade-shi, Wakayama-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|13.76|N|135|18|59.96|E|scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite = Shingi-Shingon-shu | deity = {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}} | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | functional_status = functioning | website = | founded_by = {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban|1=Kakuban}} | year_completed = 1130 | designation1 = National Treasure of Japan | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Negoro-ji'''|根来寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} located in the city of {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade|1=Iwade, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Surrounded by the sacred peaks of the {{ill|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|en|Mount Izumi Katsuragi|WD=Q11417836|lt=Katsuragi Mountains|1=Mount Izumi Katsuragi}}, the temple grounds were designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] and a [[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 2007.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164691 |title= 根来寺境内|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} ==History== In the latter half of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, from 1131, the priest {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|WD=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban|1=Kakuban}} became head of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon sect|1=Shingon Buddhism}} on {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}} and attempted to reform the sect by reuniting the Ōno (小野) and Hirosawa (広沢) branches.
